title: The Sword & Sorcery Saga - The Ship of Ishtar, by A. Merritt
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PUTrIDHNxQs

Daniel (@dungeondive) examines Abraham Merritt's The Ship of Ishtar (1924), a proto-sword & sorcery novel where American archaeologist John Kenton discovers an ancient stone block containing a miniature ship. Mysteriously teleported aboard the enchanted vessel, Kenton finds himself caught between two warring factions: the bright side devoted to the goddess of love and the dark side serving satanic evil, separated by an impassable magical barrier. The ship itself—the most fascinating element of the novel—provides intriguing dramatic tension that unfortunately resolves a quarter-way through.

Daniel finds this to be his least favorite Merritt work, hampered by purple prose, lack of narrative momentum, and an unlikeable protagonist who lacks the appeal of more compelling sword & sorcery heroes. While Sigurd, a Viking slave companion, emerges as a far more interesting character, the novel meanders through vignettes without building cohesive momentum. Though Merritt's ornate writing contains brilliant passages, the difficulty in following the convoluted narrative and the protagonist's development issues make this a challenging entry despite its conceptual merits.

----

Have you read other A. Merritt works, or does The Ship of Ishtar seem representative of his style to you?
